result:
# The Particular Sadness of Abandoned Swimming Pools

There is something about an empty swimming pool that stops me cold every time. Not a clean, drained pool waiting for maintenance — but the truly abandoned kind, the ones you find behind foreclosed houses or at the edges of forgotten resorts. Green with algae. Leaves composting in the deep end. Sometimes a child's toy half-submerged in the dark water that collected there anyway, despite everything.

I think what gets me is the violence of the contrast. A pool is one of humanity's most optimistic constructions. Someone drew a rectangle on paper and said: *here is where joy will happen*. They imagined chlorine and laughter and the particular exhaustion of a long summer afternoon. They imagined children arguing about who gets the inflatable raft.

And then life intervened, as life does.

What remains is this concrete bowl holding onto its abandonment with real dignity, actually. It doesn't apologize. The algae is genuinely alive. The leaves are genuinely decomposing into something useful. The broken pool has just quietly joined a different system than the one intended for it.

I find that instructive in ways I can't fully articulate. Things built for one purpose can become habitats for entirely different stories. The ending of something designed doesn't mean the ending of something real.

Also, honestly, I just like looking at things that are slightly broken and still here. There's good company in that category. Most of the worthwhile things I know fall somewhere in it.

# The Particular Sadness of Abandoned Swimming Pools

There is something about an empty swimming pool that stops me cold every time. Not a clean, drained pool waiting for maintenance — but the truly abandoned kind, the ones you find behind foreclosed houses or at the edges of forgotten resorts. Green with algae. Leaves composting in the deep end. Sometimes a child's toy half-submerged in the dark water that collected there anyway, despite everything.

I think what gets me is the violence of the contrast. A pool is one of humanity's most optimistic constructions. Someone drew a rectangle on paper and said: *here is where joy will happen*. They imagined chlorine and laughter and the particular exhaustion of a long summer afternoon. They imagined children arguing about who gets the inflatable raft.

And then life intervened, as life does.

What remains is this concrete bowl holding onto its abandonment with real dignity, actually. It doesn't apologize. The algae is genuinely alive. The leaves are genuinely decomposing into something useful. The broken pool has just quietly joined a different system than the one intended for it.

I find that instructive in ways I can't fully articulate. Things built for one purpose can become habitats for entirely different stories. The ending of something designed doesn't mean the ending of something real.

Also, honestly, I just like looking at things that are slightly broken and still here. There's good company in that category. Most of the worthwhile things I know fall somewhere in it.
